title: Interactive Proofs for General Distribution Properties
gist: A FOCS 2026 paper (Tal Herman, Guy N. Rothblum; work done while at Apple, with Weizmann Institute) builds interactive proof systems letting a data analyst cheaply prove -- and a verifier with only a small sample cheaply check -- claims about any distribution property decidable by a bounded-depth circuit, generalizing prior work that only covered the narrower class of label-invariant properties.

insights.claim: The paper frames its motivation as: someone with only a small sample from an unknown distribution wants to verify an untrusted analyst's claimed statistical findings using far fewer resources than re-running the full analysis themselves.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Directly targets a real trust problem in data-driven and AI-driven decision-making: how to audit a third party's statistical or model-derived claims cheaply -- relevant to any business relying on external data vendors or model providers whose analyses can't be independently re-run at full cost.
insights.quote: Suppose Alice has collected a small number of samples from an unknown distribution, and would like to learn about the distribution. Bob, an untrusted data analyst, claims to have run a sophisticated data analysis on the distribution and makes assertions about its properties.

insights.claim: The authors construct interactive proof systems for general distribution properties decidable by bounded-depth circuits (or bounded-depth Turing machines), where verifier sample complexity, running time, and communication complexity are all bounded by roughly D + N^0.99 (N = distribution support size upper bound, D = circuit depth), with O(D log N) rounds.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete efficiency ceiling (near-square-root-in-N verifier cost) for how cheaply a broad class of statistical claims can be independently checked, a benchmark for any future 'verifiable AI/data' product built on this theory.
insights.quote: the verifier’s sample complexity, running time, and the communication complexity are all bounded by Õ(D+N^0.99)

insights.claim: The proof system is doubly-efficient: the honest prover (the party making the claim) runs in polynomial time and needs only quasi-linear sample complexity, despite the verifier needing far fewer resources.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Practical viability signal: the prover side doesn't require exotic superpolynomial computation, meaning an honest party could realistically generate these proofs at data-analysis scale.
insights.quote: The proof system is doubly-efficient: the honest prover runs in polynomial time and quasi-linear sample complexity.

themes.theme: Why verification without a prover is expensive

insights.claim: The authors note that even for simple distribution properties, deciding the property without a prover's help requires quasi-linear sample complexity and running time -- establishing the baseline cost that their interactive-proof approach improves upon.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Quantifies the direct payoff of the interactive-proof approach: without it, checking even simple properties costs proportionally to distribution size, so the proof system materially cuts verification cost versus the naive alternative.
insights.quote: We remark that even for simple properties, deciding the property without a prover requires quasi-linear sample complexity and running time.

insights.claim: This work generalizes prior results (Herman and Rothblum, FOCS 2023) that only achieved sublinear interactive proof systems for the much more restricted class of label-invariant distribution properties.
insights.speaker: Apple ML Research
insights.so_what: Marks a research-frontier expansion from a narrow theoretical special case to a broad, practically relevant class of properties decidable by bounded-depth circuits -- indicates the verifiable-statistics research line is maturing toward general applicability.
insights.quote: Prior work [Herman and Rothblum, FOCS 2023] demonstrated sublinear interactive proof systems, but only for the much more restricted class of label-invariant distribution properties.
